label: ai
topic: Review of a short experimental novel set on the International Space Station
text:
I will be honest: when I picked up "Orbital" by Samantha Harvey, my expectations were calibrated to mild scepticism. A literary novel set on the International Space Station, clocking in at under two hundred pages. It sounded like the kind of concept that wins the pitch meeting and loses the reader — another entry in the growing genre of literary fiction that uses space as a metaphor for loneliness and expects the metaphor to do all the work.

I was wrong. I was so wrong that I owe Samantha Harvey a public apology, and I am offering it here.

"Orbital" follows six astronauts and cosmonauts aboard the ISS over the course of a single day — or rather, sixteen sunrises and sunsets, since the station orbits Earth every ninety minutes, compressing time in ways the novel uses to extraordinary structural effect. The crew is international: an American, a Brit, two Russians, a Japanese astronaut, and an Italian. They perform experiments, maintain the station, eat reconstituted meals, and look out the window. That is the plot.

This should not work. A novel without conventional narrative momentum, set in a metal tube where nothing goes catastrophically wrong, should be boring. Harvey seems aware of this and proceeds to dismantle the expectation with quiet confidence.

What she is doing is writing about attention. "Orbital" is a novel about what happens when you remove all the distractions of terrestrial life and force humans to exist in close proximity to the most overwhelming view in existence. The prose is lyrical without indulgence, precise without being clinical. Harvey describes the Earth from orbit with language that makes you feel the actual strangeness of what we have accomplished by putting people up there.

The characters are drawn with a light touch that is initially frustrating and gradually reveals itself as intentional. We learn about them in fragments: the American's crumbling marriage conveyed through delayed emails, the Japanese astronaut's meditation practice, the Italian's homesickness expressed through descriptions of food she cannot eat in microgravity. Harvey trusts the reader to assemble these fragments, and the trust is rewarded.

The brevity is essential. At just over a hundred and eighty pages, "Orbital" demands a single-sitting read, and this compression mirrors the experience it describes. The astronauts' day is sixteen orbits, each one a complete cycle of light and darkness. The reader's experience is similarly contained: you enter the novel's orbit and do not leave until the final page deposits you back in your own gravity, slightly dizzy.

There is a passage near the middle where Harvey describes a typhoon forming over the Pacific, seen from above. The astronauts watch it build, beautiful and terrible from their altitude, and they know that below it, people are boarding up windows and filling bathtubs with water. The astronauts can see all of it and do nothing. This tension — between cosmic perspective and human helplessness — is the novel's beating heart.

Harvey is writing about the Overview Effect, the cognitive shift reported by astronauts who see Earth from space. But she is also writing about something more subtle: the way distance clarifies. From three hundred kilometres up, national borders are invisible. Wars are invisible. What remains is the planet itself, blue and white and impossibly fragile, turning in the dark.

This could easily become sentimental, and Harvey's great achievement is that it does not. The astronauts feel awe, yes, but also grief, and the particular loneliness of seeing something beautiful you cannot touch. One reflects that the overview does not make human problems seem small. It makes them seem heartbreaking.

I picked it up a sceptic. I set it down a convert.

Rating: 4.5 out of 5 stars.
